title: Build a Multi-Container App with Docker Compose
description: Learn how to define and run a multi-container application using Docker Compose. You will build a simple web application backed by a Redis cache and a PostgreSQL database, wiring them together with a single Compose file.
prerequisites: Basic understanding of containers and images
learningObjectives: Create a docker-compose.yml file that defines multiple services
Configure networking between containers using Compose service names
Mount volumes for persistent database storage
Build a custom image alongside pre-built images in a single Compose project
Manage the lifecycle of multi-container applications with Compose commands

steps.title: Create the Project Directory
steps.description: Set up a dedicated project folder for the exercise. Keeping all files in one directory makes it easy for Docker Compose to discover them and keeps your workspace organized.

steps.title: Create the Flask Web Application
steps.description: Write a small Python Flask application that connects to both Redis (for a visitor counter) and PostgreSQL (to store visit logs). This gives you a realistic multi-service setup where the web tier depends on two backing services.
steps.codeExample:
# app.py
import os
import datetime
from flask import Flask, jsonify
import redis
import psycopg2

app = Flask(__name__)

cache = redis.Redis(host='redis', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

def get_db_connection():
    return psycopg2.connect(
        host='db',
        database='app_db',
        user='app_user',
        password='app_pass'
    )

def init_db():
    conn = get_db_connection()
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute(
        'CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS visits ('
        'id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, '
        'timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL)'
    )
    conn.commit()
    cur.close()
    conn.close()

@app.route('/')
def index():
    count = cache.incr('visitor_count')
    conn = get_db_connection()
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute('INSERT INTO visits (timestamp) VALUES (%s)', (datetime.datetime.now(),))
    conn.commit()
    cur.close()
    conn.close()
    return jsonify({
        'message': 'Welcome to the multi-container app!',
        'visitor_number': count
    })

@app.route('/health')
def health():
    return jsonify({'status': 'healthy'})

if __name__ == '__main__':
    init_db()
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000)
hints: Service names in docker-compose.yml (redis, db) are used as hostnames
Make sure the database credentials here match what you set in the Compose file later

steps.title: Create the Python Requirements File
steps.description: List the Python dependencies the Flask app needs. Docker will use this file to install packages inside the container image.
validationCriteria: requirements.txt exists in the project directory
It includes flask, redis, and psycopg2-binary

steps.title: Create the Dockerfile for the Web Service
steps.description: Write a Dockerfile that packages the Flask application into a container image. Using a slim Python base image keeps the image small while still providing everything the app needs.
steps.codeExample:
# Dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY app.py .

EXPOSE 5000

CMD ["python", "app.py"]
hints: Copying requirements.txt first and installing before copying app code improves Docker layer caching
The --no-cache-dir flag reduces the final image size
validationCriteria: Dockerfile exists in the project directory
It uses a Python slim base image
It copies and installs requirements before the application code

steps.title: Create the docker-compose.yml File
steps.description: Define all three services -- web, redis, and db -- in a single Compose file. Docker Compose automatically creates a shared network so the containers can reach each other by service name. The depends_on field ensures services start in the right order.
steps.codeExample:
# docker-compose.yml
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "5000:5000"
    depends_on:
      - redis
      - db
    environment:
      - FLASK_ENV=development

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"

  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: app_db
      POSTGRES_USER: app_user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: app_pass
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  pgdata:
hints: The volumes section at the bottom declares a named volume so database data survives container restarts
depends_on controls startup order but does not wait for the service to be ready -- the app should handle retries
validationCriteria: docker-compose.yml defines three services: web, redis, and db
The web service builds from the local Dockerfile
The db service uses a named volume for data persistence

steps.title: Start the Multi-Container Application
steps.description: Use Docker Compose to build images and start all three containers in the background. The --build flag ensures the web service image is built from the Dockerfile. The -d flag runs everything in detached mode so your terminal stays free.
steps.expectedOutput: Docker will pull the Redis and PostgreSQL images (if not cached), build the web image, create a network, and start all three containers. You should see lines ending with 'Started' for each service.
validationCriteria: All three containers are running
No error messages in the output
hints: If you see errors about ports already in use, stop any local Redis or PostgreSQL services first
Use 'docker compose logs' to check for startup errors if a container exits

steps.title: Verify All Containers Are Running
steps.description: Check that all three services are up and healthy. This step teaches you the key commands for inspecting a running Compose project.
steps.expectedOutput: docker compose ps should show three services with State 'Up'. The web logs should show the Flask development server running on port 5000.
validationCriteria: docker compose ps lists web, redis, and db services
All three services show a running state
The web service logs show no connection errors

steps.title: Test the Application
steps.description: Send HTTP requests to the Flask application and verify it communicates with both Redis and PostgreSQL. Each request increments the visitor counter in Redis and logs the visit in PostgreSQL.
steps.expectedOutput: The first curl should return a JSON response with visitor_number 1. The second should return visitor_number 2. The health endpoint should return {"status": "healthy"}.
validationCriteria: The root endpoint returns a JSON response with an incrementing visitor_number
The health endpoint returns a healthy status
No connection errors to Redis or PostgreSQL

steps.title: Explore Container Networking
steps.description: Inspect the Docker network that Compose created automatically. Understanding how containers communicate on the same network is a foundational Docker Compose concept. Every service is reachable by its name within the project network.
steps.expectedOutput: You should see a network named compose-multi-container-lab_default with all three containers attached. Each container has its own IP address on this network.
hints: The network name is derived from the project directory name plus _default
Containers resolve each other by service name (web, redis, db) via Docker's built-in DNS
validationCriteria: The default Compose network is visible in docker network ls
All three containers appear in the network inspect output

steps.title: Clean Up Resources
steps.description: Tear down the entire application stack. The --volumes flag also removes the named volume so no data is left behind. Always clean up after exercises to free up system resources.
steps.expectedOutput: Docker Compose stops and removes all containers, the default network, and the pgdata volume.
validationCriteria: All containers are stopped and removed
The pgdata volume is removed
The project network is removed

completionCriteria: All three services (web, redis, db) run simultaneously via docker compose up
The web application communicates successfully with both Redis and PostgreSQL
The visitor counter increments on each request, proving Redis connectivity
Database data persists across container restarts using a named volume
docker compose down --volumes cleanly removes all resources

troubleshooting.issue: Permission denied when running docker commands
troubleshooting.solution: Add your user to the docker group: sudo usermod -aG docker $USER, then log out and back in.
Stop any process using port 5000 (e.g., another Flask app or macOS AirPlay receiver) or change the host port mapping in docker-compose.yml to a free port like 5001:5000.

troubleshooting.issue: Web container exits immediately with a connection refused error to db
troubleshooting.solution: The database may not be ready yet. Add a short retry loop in your app or use a wait-for-it script. depends_on only controls startup order, not readiness.
Ensure you have Docker Compose V2 installed. It ships as a Docker CLI plugin with Docker Desktop. On Linux, install the docker-compose-plugin package.

troubleshooting.issue: Changes to app.py are not reflected after rebuild
troubleshooting.solution: Run docker compose up --build to force a rebuild of the web image, or add a volume mount for development (e.g., ./:/app).
